title: Magnetic Brightening and Nanoscale Imaging of Spin-Polarized Helical Edge Modes
authors: Samuel Haeuser, Richard H. J. Kim, Lin-Lin Wang, Thomas Koschny, Pedro M. Lozano, Genda Gu, Randall K. Chan, Joong-Mok Park, Martin Mootz, Liang Luo, Qiang Li, Jigang Wang
abstract: Efficient sub-10 nm electric transport remains a major challenge for nanoelectronics due to high losses and impedance mismatches in conventional Drude metals. Despite their promise of dissipationless, reflection-free conduction, topologically protected chiral edge modes remain little explored in their nanoscale spin polarized transport-particularly regarding real-space visualization, magnetic field tunability, and high-frequency edge conductivity. Here, we report magnetic brightening and nanoscale visualization of highly spin-polarizable infrared helical edge states using cryogenic magneto-infrared scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (cm-IR-sSNOM). Our measurements reveal magnetic field-induced near-field conductivity at step edges, uncovering quantum spin Hall spin-splitting modes with enhanced infrared polarizability and slightly narrowed near-field profiles. In addition, the infrared edge electrodynamic response scales nearly linearly with atomic layer number, providing compelling evidence that magnetic-field-induced gaps do not disrupt individual-layer edge states at energies of around 100 meV. These results sharply contrast with microwave and DC transport, where even small magnetically induced gaps decrease edge conduction. Magnetically tunable, topologically robust high-frequency edge modes open a pathway toward ultralow-loss nanoscale interconnects and quantum logic architectures for next-generation microelectronics, spintronics and quantum information science.
summary: This paper demonstrates the ability to visually map spin-polarized edge states in a topological insulator using infrared near-field microscopy. By applying a magnetic field, the researchers induced a 'brightening' effect that reveals the underlying spin-splitting of the edge modes. This discovery highlights a regime where topological edge conduction remains robust at high frequencies, even when magnetic fields induce gaps that would otherwise suppress DC conduction.
main_problem: The difficulty of visualizing and characterizing the nanoscale spin-polarized helical edge modes and their magnetic-field-induced spin-splitting in the weak topological insulator ZrTe5.
main_result: The researchers observed 'magnetic brightening,' where an applied magnetic field enhances near-field conductivity at step edges by lifting spin degeneracy and creating imbalanced AC currents. They also demonstrated that these high-frequency edge states remain robust and scale linearly with the number of atomic layers, unlike DC transport which is suppressed by magnetic gaps.
method: Cryogenic magneto-infrared scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (cm-IR-sSNOM) was used at temperatures down to 1.8 K and magnetic fields up to 5 T.
model_or_system: The physical system is ZrTe5, a weak topological insulator characterized by spin-polarized, counter-propagating helical edge modes at crystal step edges and domain walls.
key_observables: Near-field conductivity, infrared photocurrent oscillations, amplitude and phase contrast, and spatial width of edge modes.
important_parameters: Temperatures of 1.8-2 K, magnetic fields up to 5 T, photon energies around 100 meV, and atomic layer numbers ranging from 6 to 11.
main_assumptions: The edge states are assumed to be effectively stacked and robust in multi-layer structures due to weak van der Waals coupling.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows a schematic of the AFM probe and the mechanism of magnetic brightening; Fig 2 displays AFM topography and sSNOM images showing enhanced signal at step edges under magnetic fields; Fig 4d visualizes the spatial extent of the magnetically separated edge modes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of nanoscale transport, describes the experimental setup and ZrTe5 system, presents the discovery of magnetic brightening and spin-splitting, validates the results with layer-scaling and control experiments, and concludes with implications for future microelectronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Cryogenic magnetic infrared nano-imaging of spin-polarized edge states at crys- tal strip domains in ZrTe5. a, Representative schematic of an atomic force microscope (AFM) probe scattering infrared near-field signals above a ZrTe5 sample, operated at 1.8 K and up to 5 T. When a finite magnetic field is applied, the spin degeneracy of the edge Dirac bands is lifted, result- ing in a small magnetic gap and a spin imbalance at the Fermi energy, EF, as illustrated in the right panel. b, A magnetic field (blue arrow) is applied perpendicular to the ZrTe5 sample surface along the crystallographic b-axis, shown here for a single step edge. c, Schematics of step edges formed by stacking a...

Fig. 2 Magnetic brightening revealed by near-field signals acquired at 1.8 K under varying magnetic fields in a representative layered region of ZrTe5 a, AFM topography featuring two step edges and b, horizontal Sobel gradient of the AFM image showing sharp sloped boundaries. c-e, cm-IR-sSNOM images taken at 10.652 µm (∼116 meV) for 0 T, 3 T, and 4 T respectively. f-h, cm-IR-sSNOM images taken at 11.262 µm (∼106 meV) for 0 T, 3 T and 4 T respectively. i-k, Line cuts of AFM topography (black) and SNOM images at 10.652 µm (red) and 11.262 µm (blue) across the two step edges with heights of 5 nm and 9 nm for 0 T, 3 T and 4 T, respectively, at the gray dashed line shown in c.

Fig. 2 presents typical near-field images acquired at various magnetic fields, with the cm-IR-sSNOM setup cooled to a base temperature of 1.8 K. We focus on a clean “staircase” like structure consisting of the typical stacking step edges of ZrTe5 lay- ers. The AFM topography and the Sobel gradient in the scanning direction of the region of interest are shown in Fig. 2a-2b. The AFM images reveal step heights of approximately 9 nm and 5 nm from left to right, corresponding to stacking of 11 and 6 atomic layers, respectively. The Sobel gradient highlights the two distinct step-edge boundaries. Interestingly, the infrared near-field images reveal a pronounced magnetic- field-induced...

Fig. 3 Near-field images acquired across a sharp domain boundary in ZrTe5 at 60 K under varying magnetic fields. a, AFM topography of the sample surface. b, Horizontal Sobel gradient of the AFM image highlighting two flat plateaus separated by sloped regions that converge at a sharp domain boundary. c, 3D representation of AFM topography. Red dashed lines indicate regions of interest and mark the height variations of key surface features. d–g, SNOM images acquired at 10.652 µm (∼116 meV) under magnetic fields of 0 T, 2 T, 3 T, and 4 T, respectively. h–k, Line profiles of AFM topography (black) and SNOM signal (red) corresponding to 0 T, 2 T, 3 T, and 4 T, respectively, taken along the black...

Fig. 4 Magnetic-field and layer-number dependence of near-field edge mode signals in ZrTe5 and reference gold films. a, Near-field signals on the right (red) and left (blue) sides of the boundary, plotted as the maximum deviation from the local mean, are consistent with previously reported STM spin splitting measurements [6]. b, AFM topography; c, s3 sSNOM measurements acquired at 0 T and 3 T on a 160 nm gold film deposited over ZrTe5 for comparison. d, s3 sSNOM line scans at 3 T and 4 T, shown alongside the AFM topography line scan (black dashed line) across two step edges.
